Case Study — Real Estate Platform

Basera
Nepal

Basera Nepal is a property search platform concept designed to address a real gap in Nepal's fragmented real estate market. Existing platforms like Hamrobazar and Gharbari leave buyers with anonymous listings, no neighbourhood intelligence, and zero support for non-resident Nepalis buying from abroad. Basera is built around trust, walkability, and calm browsing — giving every user the context they need to actually decide.

View Prototype →

My Role

UX/UI, Product Thinking,
Interaction Design

Timeline

4 weeks / 2025

Tools

Figma, Google Stitch

Type

Self-initiated concept project

Early Observations

  • Existing platforms list properties without verified addresses, leaving buyers guessing location.
  • No walkability or neighbourhood context — users must independently research every locality.
  • Price listings lack transparency; commissions and fees are often hidden until the final stage.
  • Non-Resident Nepalis buying from abroad have no dedicated flow, no currency support, no legal guidance.
  • Agent profiles are absent, making it impossible to assess credibility before reaching out.

Objective / Goal

Design a property search experience that earns trust before asking for commitment. Basera Nepal required rethinking every friction point in the existing market — from anonymous listings to the complete absence of neighbourhood intelligence — and replacing them with verified data, honest pricing microcopy, and a dedicated NRN purchase flow.

Research

Competitive Market Analysis

I conducted a competitive analysis of Nepal's existing property platforms — Hamrobazar and Gharbari — alongside regional comparators like 99acres (India) and PropertyFinder (UAE). The analysis focused on trust signals, listing completeness, neighbourhood data, and how each platform handles diaspora buyers. Nepal's local platforms consistently scored lowest on verification, transparency, and mobile usability, revealing a clear structural gap rather than just a visual one.

Hamrobazar

Strengths

  • Large existing listing volume
  • Familiar to local buyers
  • Free to post

Weaknesses

  • Anonymous listings, no verification
  • No neighbourhood intelligence
  • Cluttered, low-trust interface
  • No NRN or diaspora support

Gharbari

Strengths

  • Property-specific focus
  • Agent listings available
  • Basic search filters

Weaknesses

  • Outdated visual design
  • No walkability or map data
  • Hidden commission structures
  • No currency or legal tools for NRN

99acres (India)

Strengths

  • Verified listings with agent badges
  • Locality scores and amenity data
  • Price trend history per area

Opportunity for Nepal

  • Adapt neighbourhood intelligence model
  • Apply verified badge system locally
  • Build honest pricing microcopy

Product Thinking

Understanding real buyer behaviour in Nepal's market

Rather than formal interviews, this project is grounded in direct knowledge of Nepal's property search behaviour — patterns observed across Hamrobazar usage, NRN Facebook group discussions, and the friction points Nepali buyers consistently describe when trying to buy or rent without a trusted personal contact. The design decisions map directly to documented pain points in this specific market context, not generic UX assumptions.

Key Insights

Three recurring patterns emerged across Nepal's property search context — each pointing to the same root problem: a market where buyers cannot verify anything independently.

Trust Collapse at First Contact

Buyers reach out to agents but receive no pre-contact credibility signals — no verified badge, no review, no response time estimate. The first call carries all the risk. Most users abandon before calling.

Locality Blindness

Buyers unfamiliar with Kathmandu's localities cannot compare neighbourhoods without local knowledge. No platform provides walkability, school proximity, flood risk, or commute data. Browsing feels blind.

NRN Purchase Paralysis

Non-resident Nepalis want to invest in property but face a wall of unknowns — legal eligibility, currency conversion, remote transaction processes. No platform addresses these questions directly.

POV

Property buyers in Nepal — both local and diaspora — need a way to evaluate listings independently, without relying on personal contacts or prior local knowledge. The absence of verified data, neighbourhood context, and transparent pricing forces high-stakes decisions under information poverty.

HMW

How might we design a property platform for Nepal that gives buyers — including NRNs — enough verified, contextual information to make confident decisions remotely, without a trusted local contact guiding every step?

Mapping Out User Flows

Three primary flows were mapped to cover the platform's core use cases: the local discovery flow, the property detail and lead flow, and the NRN remote purchase flow. Each was designed to reduce the number of steps before a user reaches a trust signal.

Validated Problems

  • Anonymous listings with no address verification or agent identity.
  • No neighbourhood or locality data — walkability, amenities, commute context all absent.
  • Hidden fee structures undermine buyer confidence before the first agent call.
  • NRN buyers have no dedicated flow for legal context, currency conversion, or remote transactions.
  • No feedback mechanism — expired or fraudulent listings remain live indefinitely.

Design Considerations

  • Building trust infrastructure into the listing card itself — not a separate "verification page."
  • Neighbourhood walkability as a first-class feature, not a tooltip or secondary tab.
  • Honest pricing microcopy: "No commission charged to buyers" stated inline, not in a footnote.
  • NRN flow as a distinct entry point — not a filter applied to generic listings.
  • Calm, information-dense layouts that reward browsing rather than pushing for a call too early.

High-Fidelity Design

Starting from low-fidelity sketches of the three core flows, the designs progressed through mid-fidelity wireframes that validated information hierarchy before any visual treatment was applied. High-fidelity screens prioritised the property browse page, the property detail page with the neighbourhood module, and the NRN landing flow.

Low Fidelity Wireframes

High Fidelity Designs

Usability Testing

Lightweight testing was conducted with five participants representing three user types: a local first-time buyer, a repeat local buyer, and an NRN looking to invest remotely. Each was given a scenario without guidance and asked to navigate the prototype aloud.

Test Scenarios

  • Find a 2 BHK apartment in Lazimpat with a walk score above 75.
  • Compare two properties and identify which agent to contact first.
  • As an NRN, find out whether you are legally eligible to buy property in Nepal.
  • Submit a lead form for a property without feeling like you are committing to anything.

Usability Test 1 — Findings

01

Walk score label was not immediately understood — users expected a number, not a label like "High walkability."

02

The NRN entry point was missed by two participants who did not self-identify as diaspora buyers during browsing.

03

Lead form submission felt final — participants hesitated because no "enquiry only, no commitment" framing was visible.

04

Agent response time estimate ("Usually replies in 2 hrs") was the single most reassuring element — mentioned unprompted by 4 of 5 participants.

Usability Test 2 — User Feedback

"This feels completely different from Hamrobazar. I actually trust what I'm reading here." — Local buyer, first session.

"The neighbourhood score is exactly what I needed. I don't know Lazimpat well so this told me everything." — Repeat buyer, second session.

"I finally understand what I'm allowed to buy as an NRN. No other site has ever explained this." — NRN participant, remote session.

Final Product

The second round of testing showed significantly fewer friction points across all four scenarios. The NRN flow in particular performed well once the entry point was made contextually visible within the browse experience rather than only in the nav. Final refinements tightened the neighbourhood module hierarchy and added agent response time estimates to all listing cards.

  • Walk score redesigned as a numeric + descriptor pair across all listing cards and detail pages.
  • Lead form microcopy updated — "enquiry only, no commitment" visible before submit on every form instance.
  • NRN entry point surfaced contextually within browse results for properties tagged "NRN Ready."
  • Agent response time estimate added to all agent cards as a trust signal.

Final Product — Basera Nepal

View Full Prototype →

Project Takeaways

1) Trust is structural, not cosmetic

Every trust signal in Basera — verified badges, agent response times, honest pricing microcopy — had to live in the product flow, not on a separate "about" page. When trust is embedded at the moment of decision, it changes behaviour. Participants who previously hesitated to submit a lead form did so without friction once the microcopy was present. This is a design decision, not a copywriting decision.

2) Designing for a context you know is a design asset

Because this project is rooted in a real, specific market context — Nepal's property search behaviour, the NRN diaspora, Hamrobazar's known failure modes — every design decision had a grounded rationale. The neighbourhood walkability feature wasn't a nice-to-have borrowed from 99acres; it solved a documented problem specific to buyers unfamiliar with Kathmandu's localities. Context specificity makes design defensible.

3) Entry points shape who the product serves

The NRN flow was fully designed but nearly invisible in the first test round because its entry point was only in the top nav. Moving it into the browse experience — surfaced contextually for NRN-tagged listings — changed who felt the product was "for them." This was the single biggest learning: a feature that isn't findable in context doesn't exist for the user who needs it most.
